1:
"And Samson went down to Timnath, and saw a woman in Timnath of the daughters of the Philistines." The witnesses note Samson going down to Timnath and there seeing a Philistine woman. The witnesses note that Gill notes Timnath fell by lot to Judah, then to Dan, and was now in the hands of the Philistines. The witnesses note that he saw a woman of the daughters of the Philistines. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the Samson went down — to Timnath; the among the Philistines — the enemy; the saw a woman — the unguarded eye; the keep the eyes — i made a covenant with mine eyes (Job 31:1).

The witnesses agree the story of Samson opens with a fateful sight — "Samson went down to Timnath, and saw a woman... of the Philistines": his going down among the uncircumcised, and his eye fixing on a daughter of the enemy, set the course of his troubled history; the leading of the eye toward a forbidden object shows the peril of the unguarded sight; it teaches that the people of God must keep watch over their eyes, lest the look lead the heart astray, "I made a covenant with mine eyes; why then should I think upon a maid?" (Job 31:1), Samson seeing the woman of Timnath.

So the witnesses make the verse saw a woman: The witnesses note the Samson went down: to Timnath. The witnesses note the among the Philistines: the enemy. The witnesses note the saw a woman: the unguarded eye. The witnesses note the keep the eyes: I made a covenant with mine eyes (Job 31:1). The expositors observe the Samson went down: to Timnath. The expositors observe the among the Philistines: the enemy. The expositors observe the saw a woman: the unguarded eye. The expositors observe the keep the eyes: I made a covenant with mine eyes (Job 31:1). The believer is taught to mark the Samson went down, the among the Philistines, the saw a woman, the keep the eyes, and to own the providence that brings sweetness out of the strong. The verse thus records that Samson went down to Timnath and saw a Philistine woman — the peril of the unguarded sight leading the heart astray, the people of God to keep watch over their eyes, making a covenant with them (Job 31:1).
2:
"And he came up, and told his father and his mother, and said, I have seen a woman in Timnath... now therefore get her for me to wife." The witnesses note Samson telling his parents and asking them to get him the Philistine woman. The witnesses note that Gill notes he told them, desiring their approbation and consent, acting a dutiful part, an example to children to advise with parents. The witnesses note that he said, get her for me to wife. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the told his father and mother — his desire; the I have seen a woman — at Timnath; the get her for me — to wife; the children advise with parents — children, obey your parents (Eph. 6:1).

The witnesses agree Samson, though set on a forbidden choice, yet sought his parents' consent — "he... told his father and his mother... get her for me to wife": Gill marks that in seeking their approval he "acted a dutiful part," a pattern for children to advise with parents in marriage; yet his demand, "get her for me," showed a will already fixed on what was unlawful; it teaches both the duty of children to seek their parents' counsel in marriage, and the danger of asking consent for what is already wrongly resolved, "children, obey your parents in the Lord" (Eph. 6:1), Samson telling his parents to get him the woman.

So the witnesses make the verse get her for me: The witnesses note the told his father and mother: his desire. The witnesses note the I have seen a woman: at Timnath. The witnesses note the get her for me: to wife. The witnesses note the children advise with parents: children, obey your parents (Eph. 6:1). The expositors observe the told his father and mother: his desire. The expositors observe the I have seen a woman: at Timnath. The expositors observe the get her for me: to wife. The expositors observe the children advise with parents: children, obey your parents (Eph. 6:1). The believer is taught to mark the told his father and mother, the I have seen a woman, the get her for me, the children advise with parents, and to own the providence that brings sweetness out of the strong. The verse thus records that Samson told his parents and asked them to get him the woman — the duty to seek parents' counsel in marriage, yet the danger of asking consent for what is already wrongly resolved, children obeying their parents in the Lord (Eph. 6:1).
3:
"Then his father and his mother said unto him, Is there never a woman among the daughters of thy brethren... that thou goest to take a wife of the uncircumcised Philistines?..." The witnesses note the parents objecting to a marriage with the uncircumcised Philistines. The witnesses note that Gill sets their expected objection. The witnesses note that Clarke notes to marry any not of the Israelitish stock was contrary to the law. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the is there never a woman — among thy brethren; the a wife of the Philistines — the uncircumcised; the contrary to the law — their objection; the not unequally yoked — be not unequally yoked (2 Cor. 6:14).

The witnesses agree the parents rightly objected to the unequal and forbidden match — "Is there never a woman among... thy brethren, that thou goest to take a wife of the uncircumcised Philistines?": Clarke notes that to marry outside Israel was "contrary to the law" (Deut. 7:3); the godly remonstrance of the parents shows their care for the law of God and their son's good; it teaches that the people of God are not to be unequally yoked with unbelievers, and that parents do well to warn their children against such alliances, "be ye not unequally yoked together with unbelievers" (2 Cor. 6:14), the parents objecting to the Philistine wife.

So the witnesses make the verse the uncircumcised Philistines: The witnesses note the is there never a woman: among thy brethren. The witnesses note the a wife of the Philistines: the uncircumcised. The witnesses note the contrary to the law: their objection. The witnesses note the not unequally yoked: be not unequally yoked (2 Cor. 6:14). The expositors observe the contrary to the law: their objection. The expositors observe the not unequally yoked: be not unequally yoked (2 Cor. 6:14). The believer is taught to mark the is there never a woman, the a wife of the Philistines, the contrary to the law, the not unequally yoked, and to own the providence that brings sweetness out of the strong. The verse thus records that the parents objected to a wife of the uncircumcised Philistines — their care for the law of God, the people of God not to be unequally yoked with unbelievers, be ye not unequally yoked together with unbelievers (2 Cor. 6:14).
4:
"But his father and his mother knew not that it was of the LORD, that he sought an occasion against the Philistines: for at that time the Philistines had dominion over Israel." The witnesses note that the marriage was overruled of the LORD, who sought an occasion against the Philistines. The witnesses note that Gill notes Samson knew it was of the Lord, his desire not arising from carnal affection merely. The witnesses note that the Philistines had dominion over Israel. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the they knew not — the hidden purpose; the it was of the LORD — his providence; the an occasion against the Philistines — the deliverance; the God overrules all — the wrath of man shall praise thee (Ps. 76:10).

The witnesses agree a deep providence lay behind the matter — "his father and his mother knew not that it was of the LORD, that he sought an occasion against the Philistines": God was overruling Samson's inclination to bring about the deliverance of Israel from their oppressors; the working of God's secret purpose through the very course men would condemn shows the mystery of his providence, which turns even men's faults to his ends without excusing them; it teaches that the LORD governs all things to his appointed purpose, the wrath and folly of man made to praise him, "surely the wrath of man shall praise thee" (Ps. 76:10), the marriage of the LORD to seek occasion against the Philistines.

So the witnesses make the verse it was of the LORD: The witnesses note the they knew not: the hidden purpose. The witnesses note the it was of the LORD: his providence. The witnesses note the an occasion against the Philistines: the deliverance. The witnesses note the God overrules all: the wrath of man shall praise thee (Ps. 76:10). The expositors observe the an occasion against the Philistines: the deliverance. The expositors observe the God overrules all: the wrath of man shall praise thee (Ps. 76:10). The believer is taught to mark the they knew not, the it was of the LORD, the an occasion against the Philistines, the God overrules all, and to own the providence that brings sweetness out of the strong. The verse thus records that the marriage was of the LORD, who sought an occasion against the Philistines — the mystery of providence turning men's course to his ends, God governing all to his purpose, the wrath of man made to praise him (Ps. 76:10).
5:
"Then went Samson down, and his father and his mother, to Timnath, and came to the vineyards of Timnath: and, behold, a young lion roared against him." The witnesses note Samson and his parents coming to Timnath, where a young lion roared against him. The witnesses note that Gill notes the parents were prevailed upon to go, his affections being so set on the marriage. The witnesses note that a young lion roared against him in the vineyards. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the went down to Timnath — with his parents; the the vineyards — his way; the a young lion — roared against him; the the roaring foe — the devil as a roaring lion (1 Pet. 5:8).

The witnesses agree the journey brought a sudden peril — "a young lion roared against him": as Samson came to the vineyards, the beast sprang upon him; the meeting of the danger on the way to his unwise errand shows how trouble may beset us even in our wandering paths; yet, as the next verse tells, God's strength would meet the danger; it teaches that the people of God may encounter the roaring foe in unexpected places, and must look to the LORD's help against the lion that seeks to devour, "your adversary the devil, as a roaring lion, walketh about, seeking whom he may devour" (1 Pet. 5:8), the young lion roaring against Samson.

So the witnesses make the verse a young lion roared: The witnesses note the went down to Timnath: with his parents. The witnesses note the the vineyards: his way. The witnesses note the a young lion: roared against him. The witnesses note the the roaring foe: the devil as a roaring lion (1 Pet. 5:8). The expositors observe the went down to Timnath: with his parents. The expositors observe the the vineyards: his way. The expositors observe the a young lion: roared against him. The expositors observe the the roaring foe: the devil as a roaring lion (1 Pet. 5:8). The believer is taught to mark the went down to Timnath, the the vineyards, the a young lion, the the roaring foe, and to own the providence that brings sweetness out of the strong. The verse thus records that a young lion roared against Samson in the vineyards — trouble besetting even our wandering paths, the people of God meeting the roaring foe, the adversary as a roaring lion seeking whom he may devour (1 Pet. 5:8).
6:
"And the Spirit of the LORD came mightily upon him, and he rent him as he would have rent a kid, and he had nothing in his hand..." The witnesses note the Spirit of the LORD coming mightily on Samson, who rent the lion bare-handed. The witnesses note that Gill notes the Spirit of might from the Lord, inspiring courage and increasing his bodily strength. The witnesses note that he rent the lion as a kid, with nothing in his hand. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the Spirit came mightily — upon him; the rent him as a kid — bare-handed; the nothing in his hand — the Spirit's power; the strength from the Spirit — strong in the power of his might (Eph. 6:10).

The witnesses agree the strength to slay the lion was wholly of God — "the Spirit of the LORD came mightily upon him, and he rent him as... a kid": with no weapon in his hand, Samson tore the beast asunder by the power the Spirit gave; the bestowing of such might shows that Samson's strength was no natural endowment but a gift of the Spirit of the LORD; it teaches that all the power of God's servants is from his Spirit, the weak made mighty for the work he gives, "be strong in the Lord, and in the power of his might" (Eph. 6:10); the lion rent by the Spirit's power, a token of the strength that would overthrow the Philistines.

So the witnesses make the verse rent the lion: The witnesses note the the Spirit came mightily: upon him. The witnesses note the rent him as a kid: bare-handed. The witnesses note the nothing in his hand: the Spirit's power. The witnesses note the strength from the Spirit: strong in the power of his might (Eph. 6:10). The expositors observe the the Spirit came mightily: upon him. The expositors observe the rent him as a kid: bare-handed. The expositors observe the nothing in his hand: the Spirit's power. The expositors observe the strength from the Spirit: strong in the power of his might (Eph. 6:10). The believer is taught to mark the the Spirit came mightily, the rent him as a kid, the nothing in his hand, the strength from the Spirit, and to own the providence that brings sweetness out of the strong. The verse thus records that the Spirit of the LORD came mightily on Samson, who rent the lion bare-handed — his strength a gift of the Spirit, the weak made mighty for God's work, strong in the Lord and in the power of his might (Eph. 6:10).
7:
"And he went down, and talked with the woman; and she pleased Samson well." The witnesses note Samson talking with the woman, who pleased him well. The witnesses note that Gill notes he entered conversation to judge her other qualifications besides outward form. The witnesses note that she pleased Samson well. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the talked with the woman — conversed; the she pleased him well — his liking; the founded on the eye — the desire of the flesh; the not ruled by the eye — the lust of the eyes is not of the Father (1 John 2:16).

The witnesses agree Samson, having met and conversed with the woman, was the more set upon her — "he... talked with the woman; and she pleased Samson well": his liking, founded chiefly on what pleased the eye and fancy, fixed his purpose; the repeating that "she pleased him well" marks how the desire of the flesh governed his choice; it teaches that the people of God must not let the pleasing of the eye and the passions rule their weightiest choices, but seek what pleases God, "the lust of the flesh, and the lust of the eyes... is not of the Father" (1 John 2:16), the woman pleasing Samson well.

So the witnesses make the verse she pleased him well: The witnesses note the talked with the woman: conversed. The witnesses note the she pleased him well: his liking. The witnesses note the founded on the eye: the desire of the flesh. The witnesses note the not ruled by the eye: the lust of the eyes is not of the Father (1 John 2:16). The expositors observe the not ruled by the eye: the lust of the eyes is not of the Father (1 John 2:16). The believer is taught to mark the talked with the woman, the she pleased him well, the founded on the eye, the not ruled by the eye, and to own the providence that brings sweetness out of the strong. The verse thus records that Samson talked with the woman, who pleased him well — the desire of the flesh governing his choice, the people of God not to let the pleasing of the eye rule their weightiest choices, the lust of the eyes not of the Father (1 John 2:16).
8:
"And after a time he returned to take her, and he turned aside to see the carcase of the lion: and, behold, there was a swarm of bees and honey in the carcase of the lion." The witnesses note Samson finding a swarm of bees and honey in the carcase of the lion he had slain. The witnesses note that Gill notes matters being settled and the espousals made, he returned at the time for the marriage. The witnesses note that there was a swarm of bees and honey in the carcase. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the returned to take her — the marriage; the the carcase of the lion — his former victory; the bees and honey — sweetness from death; the good out of evil — death's sting drawn, victory given (1 Cor. 15:55-57).

The witnesses agree a marvel met him in the place of his former victory — "a swarm of bees and honey in the carcase of the lion": where death and fierceness had been, now was sweetness and food; the bringing of honey out of the dead lion is a striking figure, the ground of the riddle to come — that out of the strong came forth sweetness; it teaches that God brings good out of evil and sweetness out of the bitter, life out of death, a faint shadow of the greatest sweetness drawn from the conquered power of death by Christ the Lion of Judah, "O death, where is thy sting?... thanks be to God, which giveth us the victory" (1 Cor. 15:55-57), the honey in the lion's carcase.

So the witnesses make the verse honey in the carcase: The witnesses note the returned to take her: the marriage. The witnesses note the the carcase of the lion: his former victory. The witnesses note the bees and honey: sweetness from death. The witnesses note the good out of evil: death's sting drawn, victory given (1 Cor. 15:55-57). The expositors observe the returned to take her: the marriage. The expositors observe the the carcase of the lion: his former victory. The expositors observe the bees and honey: sweetness from death. The expositors observe the good out of evil: death's sting drawn, victory given (1 Cor. 15:55-57). The believer is taught to mark the returned to take her, the the carcase of the lion, the bees and honey, the good out of evil, and to own the providence that brings sweetness out of the strong. The verse thus records that Samson found honey in the carcase of the lion — God bringing sweetness out of the bitter and life out of death, a shadow of the sweetness drawn from death's conquered power by Christ, who gives us the victory (1 Cor. 15:55-57).
9:
"And he took thereof in his hands, and went on eating, and came to his father and mother, and he gave them, and they did eat: but he told not them that he had taken the honey out of the carcase of the lion." The witnesses note Samson taking the honey, sharing it with his parents, but not telling its source. The witnesses note that Gill notes Josephus says he took pieces of the honeycomb and ate as he went. The witnesses note that he told them not he had taken it from the lion's carcase. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the took the honey — in his hands; the gave to his parents — they did eat; the told them not — its source; the discretion — a prudent man concealeth knowledge (Prov. 12:23).

The witnesses agree Samson kept to himself the secret of the honey — "he gave them, and they did eat: but he told not them that he had taken the honey out of the carcase": he shared the sweetness but concealed its origin, partly that his parents, mindful of his Nazarite separation, might not scruple at honey taken from a dead body; the keeping back of the matter would furnish his riddle; it teaches that there are things wisely kept to oneself for a season, yet warns that concealment touching the dead may have brushed against his Nazarite vow; the believer is to walk in the light, yet with discretion, "a prudent man concealeth knowledge" (Prov. 12:23), Samson telling not the source of the honey.

So the witnesses make the verse told not the source: The witnesses note the took the honey: in his hands. The witnesses note the gave to his parents: they did eat. The witnesses note the told them not: its source. The witnesses note the discretion: a prudent man concealeth knowledge (Prov. 12:23). The expositors observe the took the honey: in his hands. The expositors observe the gave to his parents: they did eat. The expositors observe the told them not: its source. The expositors observe the discretion: a prudent man concealeth knowledge (Prov. 12:23). The believer is taught to mark the took the honey, the gave to his parents, the told them not, the discretion, and to own the providence that brings sweetness out of the strong. The verse thus records that Samson shared the honey but concealed its source — things wisely kept for a season, yet a brush against his Nazarite separation, the believer walking in light with discretion, a prudent man concealing knowledge (Prov. 12:23).
10:
"So his father went down unto the woman: and Samson made there a feast; for so used the young men to do." The witnesses note Samson making a wedding feast at Timnath, as was the custom. The witnesses note that Gill notes the father went down about the business of the woman, the consummation of the marriage. The witnesses note that Samson made a feast, as the young men used to do. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the his father went down — to the woman; the Samson made a feast — the custom; the so used the young men — the usage; the beware the company — evil communications corrupt good manners (1 Cor. 15:33).

The witnesses agree the marriage proceeded with the customary feast — "Samson made there a feast; for so used the young men to do": following the usage of the time, he kept a seven-day feast for the wedding; the conforming to the common custom, in itself harmless, was set in the midst of an unequal alliance among the enemies of God's people; it teaches that the people of God may follow lawful customs, yet must beware the company and alliances in which they keep them, lest the feast be among the ungodly, "evil communications corrupt good manners" (1 Cor. 15:33), Samson making the wedding feast at Timnath.

So the witnesses make the verse made a feast: The witnesses note the his father went down: to the woman. The witnesses note the Samson made a feast: the custom. The witnesses note the so used the young men: the usage. The witnesses note the beware the company: evil communications corrupt good manners (1 Cor. 15:33). The expositors observe the his father went down: to the woman. The expositors observe the Samson made a feast: the custom. The expositors observe the so used the young men: the usage. The expositors observe the beware the company: evil communications corrupt good manners (1 Cor. 15:33). The believer is taught to mark the his father went down, the Samson made a feast, the so used the young men, the beware the company, and to own the providence that brings sweetness out of the strong. The verse thus records that Samson made a wedding feast as was the custom — lawful customs to be kept, yet beware the company and alliances among the ungodly, evil communications corrupting good manners (1 Cor. 15:33).
11:
"And it came to pass, when they saw him, that they brought thirty companions to be with him." The witnesses note thirty companions brought to be with Samson at the feast. The witnesses note that Gill notes the Philistines of Timnath, when they saw he was come to consummate his marriage, brought them. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set the thirty companions. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the when they saw him — the Philistines; the thirty companions — set with him; the a show of honour — to watch him; the compassed with the world — i have hated the congregation of evil doers (Ps. 26:4-5).

The witnesses agree the Philistines surrounded Samson with their own — "they brought thirty companions to be with him": under show of honour, they set thirty of their men about him, perhaps to watch and guard against so mighty a stranger; the placing of the enemy's company around the man of God shows the danger of the alliance into which his marriage had drawn him; it teaches that he who joins himself to the ungodly finds himself compassed with their company, and that the unequal yoke surrounds the believer with the world, "I have not sat with vain persons... I have hated the congregation of evil doers" (Ps. 26:4-5), the thirty companions set with Samson.

So the witnesses make the verse thirty companions: The witnesses note the when they saw him: the Philistines. The witnesses note the thirty companions: set with him. The witnesses note the a show of honour: to watch him. The witnesses note the compassed with the world: I have hated the congregation of evil doers (Ps. 26:4-5). The expositors observe the when they saw him: the Philistines. The expositors observe the thirty companions: set with him. The expositors observe the a show of honour: to watch him. The expositors observe the compassed with the world: I have hated the congregation of evil doers (Ps. 26:4-5). The believer is taught to mark the when they saw him, the thirty companions, the a show of honour, the compassed with the world, and to beware the snare of the unequal yoke. The verse thus records that thirty Philistine companions were set with Samson — the danger of the alliance compassing him with the enemy's company, the unequal yoke surrounding the believer with the world, hating the congregation of evil doers (Ps. 26:4-5).
12:
"And Samson said unto them, I will now put forth a riddle unto you: if ye can certainly declare it me within the seven days of the feast, and find it out, then I will give you thirty sheets and thirty change of garments:" The witnesses note Samson proposing a riddle to his thirty companions, with a wager of garments. The witnesses note that Gill notes he said it to his thirty companions, very likely on the first day of the feast. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set the riddle and the wager. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the I will put forth a riddle — to the company; the within seven days — the term; the thirty sheets and garments — the wager; the dark sayings of the wise — the words of the wise and their dark sayings (Prov. 1:6).

The witnesses agree Samson set a riddle before the company with a wager — "I will now put forth a riddle... then I will give you thirty sheets and thirty change of garments": the matching of wits in a dark saying was a custom of such feasts, and Samson, sure of his secret, ventured the wager; the proposing of the riddle would become the occasion of conflict with the Philistines, as God intended; it teaches that God can turn even a feast's diversion to his purpose, and that the dark sayings of the wise have their place, "to understand a proverb, and the interpretation; the words of the wise, and their dark sayings" (Prov. 1:6), Samson putting forth his riddle.

So the witnesses make the verse put forth a riddle: The witnesses note the I will put forth a riddle: to the company. The witnesses note the within seven days: the term. The witnesses note the thirty sheets and garments: the wager. The witnesses note the dark sayings of the wise: the words of the wise and their dark sayings (Prov. 1:6). The expositors observe the I will put forth a riddle: to the company. The expositors observe the within seven days: the term. The expositors observe the thirty sheets and garments: the wager. The expositors observe the dark sayings of the wise: the words of the wise and their dark sayings (Prov. 1:6). The believer is taught to mark the I will put forth a riddle, the within seven days, the thirty sheets and garments, the dark sayings of the wise, and to beware the snare of the unequal yoke. The verse thus records that Samson proposed a riddle with a wager to his companions — the matching of wits a feast's custom, God turning even the diversion to his purpose, the dark sayings of the wise having their place (Prov. 1:6).
13:
"But if ye cannot declare it me, then shall ye give me thirty sheets and thirty change of garments. And they said unto him, Put forth thy riddle, that we may hear it." The witnesses note the companions agreeing to the wager and asking to hear the riddle. The witnesses note that Gill notes if they could not explain it in the time allowed. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set their reply, Put forth thy riddle. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the if ye cannot declare it — the terms; the thirty sheets and garments — the wager; the put forth thy riddle — they agreed; the the world's confidence — the wisdom of this world is foolishness (1 Cor. 3:19).

The witnesses agree the bargain was struck on even terms — "if ye cannot declare it me, then shall ye give me thirty sheets... Put forth thy riddle": confident of their wit, the Philistines accepted the wager, eager to hear and solve it; the readiness of the worldly to match their cunning shows their confidence in their own understanding; yet they would gain the answer not by wit but by treachery; it teaches that the cleverness of the world, trusting in itself, often resorts to crooked means when honest skill fails, "the wisdom of this world is foolishness with God" (1 Cor. 3:19), the companions accepting the riddle.

So the witnesses make the verse put forth thy riddle: The witnesses note the if ye cannot declare it: the terms. The witnesses note the thirty sheets and garments: the wager. The witnesses note the put forth thy riddle: they agreed. The witnesses note the the world's confidence: the wisdom of this world is foolishness (1 Cor. 3:19). The expositors observe the if ye cannot declare it: the terms. The expositors observe the thirty sheets and garments: the wager. The expositors observe the put forth thy riddle: they agreed. The expositors observe the the world's confidence: the wisdom of this world is foolishness (1 Cor. 3:19). The believer is taught to mark the if ye cannot declare it, the thirty sheets and garments, the put forth thy riddle, the the world's confidence, and to beware the snare of the unequal yoke. The verse thus records that the companions accepted the wager and asked to hear the riddle — the worldly confident in their own wit, yet to gain the answer by treachery, the wisdom of this world foolishness with God (1 Cor. 3:19).
14:
"And he said unto them, Out of the eater came forth meat, and out of the strong came forth sweetness. And they could not in three days expound the riddle." The witnesses note Samson's riddle of the honey from the lion, which they could not solve. The witnesses note that Gill notes out of a devouring eater, such as the lion, came forth honey. The witnesses note that they could not in three days expound it. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the out of the eater — came meat; the out of the strong — came sweetness; the the honey from the lion — the dark saying; the sweetness from death — through death he destroyed death's power (Heb. 2:14).

The witnesses agree the riddle drew its meaning from the honey in the slain lion — "Out of the eater came forth meat, and out of the strong came forth sweetness": from the devourer came food, from the fierce lion sweetness; the dark saying, which the Philistines could not unriddle, holds a deep gospel truth, for out of the Strong One, Christ the Lion of Judah, who through death destroyed him that had the power of death, comes forth the sweetness of life and salvation; it teaches that God brings sweetness out of strength and life out of death, the riddle a shadow of the cross, "that through death he might destroy him that had the power of death" (Heb. 2:14), the honey from the strong.

So the witnesses make the verse out of the strong: The witnesses note the out of the eater: came meat. The witnesses note the out of the strong: came sweetness. The witnesses note the the honey from the lion: the dark saying. The witnesses note the sweetness from death: through death he destroyed death's power (Heb. 2:14). The expositors observe the out of the eater: came meat. The expositors observe the out of the strong: came sweetness. The expositors observe the the honey from the lion: the dark saying. The expositors observe the sweetness from death: through death he destroyed death's power (Heb. 2:14). The believer is taught to mark the out of the eater, the out of the strong, the the honey from the lion, the sweetness from death, and to beware the snare of the unequal yoke. The verse thus records that Samson's riddle drew sweetness from the strong — a shadow of the gospel, that out of the Strong One who conquered death comes the sweetness of life, through death destroying him that had the power of death (Heb. 2:14).
15:
"And it came to pass on the seventh day, that they said unto Samson's wife, Entice thy husband, that he may declare unto us the riddle, lest we burn thee and thy father's house with fire..." The witnesses note the Philistines threatening Samson's wife to entice him to reveal the riddle. The witnesses note that Gill notes they applied to his wife some time before the seventh day, pressing her hard. The witnesses note that they threatened to burn her and her father's house. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the on the seventh day — the threat; the entice thy husband — to betray him; the lest we burn thee — the menace; the treachery of the ungodly — they bend their tongues for lies (Jer. 9:3).

The witnesses agree the Philistines turned to threats and treachery — "Entice thy husband... lest we burn thee and thy father's house": unable to solve the riddle by wit, they forced the wife by fear to betray her husband; the using of menace to extort the secret shows the cruelty and faithlessness of the ungodly; it teaches that the world, when it cannot prevail by fair means, resorts to threats and treachery, and that the people of God are surrounded by such snares when yoked with the enemy, "they bend their tongues like their bow for lies" (Jer. 9:3), the Philistines threatening Samson's wife.

So the witnesses make the verse entice thy husband: The witnesses note the on the seventh day: the threat. The witnesses note the entice thy husband: to betray him. The witnesses note the lest we burn thee: the menace. The witnesses note the treachery of the ungodly: they bend their tongues for lies (Jer. 9:3). The expositors observe the on the seventh day: the threat. The expositors observe the entice thy husband: to betray him. The expositors observe the lest we burn thee: the menace. The expositors observe the treachery of the ungodly: they bend their tongues for lies (Jer. 9:3). The believer is taught to mark the on the seventh day, the entice thy husband, the lest we burn thee, the treachery of the ungodly, and to beware the snare of the unequal yoke. The verse thus records that the Philistines threatened the wife to entice Samson — the world resorting to threats and treachery when fair means fail, the people of God surrounded by snares when yoked with the enemy, tongues bent for lies (Jer. 9:3).
16:
"And Samson's wife wept before him, and said, Thou dost but hate me, and lovest me not... And he said unto her, Behold, I have not told it my father nor my mother, and shall I tell it thee?" The witnesses note the wife weeping and pleading, accusing Samson of not loving her. The witnesses note that Gill notes she came to get the explanation, thinking her tears would move him. The witnesses note that he said, I have not told my father nor mother, and shall I tell thee? The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the wept before him — her tears; the thou dost but hate me — the reproach; the I have not told my parents — his resistance; the worn down by tears — her end is bitter (Prov. 5:3-4).

The witnesses agree the wife used tears and reproach to draw out the secret — "Samson's wife wept before him... Thou dost but hate me": she made his keeping of the riddle a proof that he loved her not, pressing him with weeping; Samson at first resisted, that he had not told even his parents; the weeping and false accusation of the wife show the arts by which the ungodly wear down the resolve of God's people; it teaches that the importunity and tears of an unbelieving partner are a snare, and that the believer must beware the wearing pressure that seeks to draw out what should be kept, "the lips of a strange woman drop as an honeycomb... but her end is bitter" (Prov. 5:3-4), the wife weeping before Samson.

So the witnesses make the verse wept before him: The witnesses note the wept before him: her tears. The witnesses note the thou dost but hate me: the reproach. The witnesses note the I have not told my parents: his resistance. The witnesses note the worn down by tears: her end is bitter (Prov. 5:3-4). The expositors observe the wept before him: her tears. The expositors observe the thou dost but hate me: the reproach. The expositors observe the I have not told my parents: his resistance. The expositors observe the worn down by tears: her end is bitter (Prov. 5:3-4). The believer is taught to mark the wept before him, the thou dost but hate me, the I have not told my parents, the worn down by tears, and to beware the snare of the unequal yoke. The verse thus records that the wife wept and accused Samson to draw out the riddle — the arts by which the ungodly wear down God's people, the importunity of an unbelieving partner a snare, her lips dropping as honeycomb but her end bitter (Prov. 5:3-4).
17:
"And she wept before him the seven days, while their feast lasted: and it came to pass on the seventh day, that he told her, because she lay sore upon him: and she told the riddle to the children of her people." The witnesses note the wife weeping all the days until Samson yielded, then betraying the riddle. The witnesses note that Gill notes she wept the remaining days, from the fourth to the seventh, lying sore upon him. The witnesses note that he told her, and she told the riddle to her people. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the wept the seven days — unceasing; the she lay sore upon him — worn down; the he told her — yielded; the the betrayal — give not that which is holy to dogs (Matt. 7:6).

The witnesses agree the constant weeping at last broke Samson down — "she wept before him the seven days... he told her, because she lay sore upon him: and she told the riddle": the unceasing pressure wore out his resolve, and once he told her, she betrayed him to her people; the yielding of Samson to importunity, and the treachery that followed, show the peril of giving way to the persistent urging of the ungodly; it teaches that to yield the secret of our strength or counsel to an untrustworthy heart is to be betrayed, and warns against the wearing importunity that ends in our undoing, "give not that which is holy unto the dogs" (Matt. 7:6), Samson yielding and the wife betraying the riddle.

So the witnesses make the verse she lay sore upon him: The witnesses note the wept the seven days: unceasing. The witnesses note the she lay sore upon him: worn down. The witnesses note the he told her: yielded. The witnesses note the the betrayal: give not that which is holy to dogs (Matt. 7:6). The expositors observe the wept the seven days: unceasing. The expositors observe the she lay sore upon him: worn down. The expositors observe the he told her: yielded. The expositors observe the the betrayal: give not that which is holy to dogs (Matt. 7:6). The believer is taught to mark the wept the seven days, the she lay sore upon him, the he told her, the the betrayal, and to beware the snare of the unequal yoke. The verse thus records that the wife's constant weeping broke Samson down, and she betrayed the riddle — the peril of yielding to the persistent urging of the ungodly, the secret given to an untrustworthy heart betrayed, not giving what is holy to dogs (Matt. 7:6).
18:
"And the men of the city said unto him on the seventh day before the sun went down, What is sweeter than honey? and what is stronger than a lion?... If ye had not plowed with my heifer, ye had not found out my riddle." The witnesses note the men solving the riddle, and Samson knowing they had used his wife. The witnesses note that Gill notes they answered before sundown, soon enough to free themselves from the obligation. The witnesses note that Samson said, if ye had not plowed with my heifer. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the what is sweeter than honey — the answer; the what is stronger than a lion — solved; the plowed with my heifer — the treachery; the ill-gotten no victory — bread of deceit afterward gravel (Prov. 20:17).

The witnesses agree Samson saw at once that the answer was got by treachery — "If ye had not plowed with my heifer, ye had not found out my riddle": the men solved the riddle only because his wife had betrayed it, and Samson exposed their underhand means with a sharp proverb; the unmasking of their treachery shows that ill-gotten gains are no true victory; it teaches that the people of God may discern and expose the crooked dealings of the world, and that what is won by treachery is shameful, not honourable, "bread of deceit is sweet to a man; but afterwards his mouth shall be filled with gravel" (Prov. 20:17), Samson exposing the plowing with his heifer.

So the witnesses make the verse plowed with my heifer: The witnesses note the what is sweeter than honey: the answer. The witnesses note the what is stronger than a lion: solved. The witnesses note the plowed with my heifer: the treachery. The witnesses note the ill-gotten no victory: bread of deceit afterward gravel (Prov. 20:17). The expositors observe the what is sweeter than honey: the answer. The expositors observe the what is stronger than a lion: solved. The expositors observe the plowed with my heifer: the treachery. The expositors observe the ill-gotten no victory: bread of deceit afterward gravel (Prov. 20:17). The believer is taught to mark the what is sweeter than honey, the what is stronger than a lion, the plowed with my heifer, the ill-gotten no victory, and to beware the snare of the unequal yoke. The verse thus records that Samson exposed that the riddle was solved by his wife's betrayal — ill-gotten gains no true victory, the crooked dealings of the world discerned and shamed, bread of deceit afterward filling the mouth with gravel (Prov. 20:17).
19:
"And the Spirit of the LORD came upon him, and he went down to Ashkelon, and slew thirty men of them, and took their spoil, and gave change of garments unto them which expounded the riddle..." The witnesses note the Spirit coming on Samson, who slew thirty at Ashkelon to pay the wager. The witnesses note that Gill notes the Spirit of might from the Lord filled him with zeal and courage and increased his strength. The witnesses note that he gave the changes to them that expounded the riddle. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the Spirit came upon him — empowered; the went down to Ashkelon — the stroke; the slew thirty men — took their spoil; the the affront overruled — he taketh the wise in their craftiness (Job 5:13).

The witnesses agree the Spirit again empowered Samson, turning the wager into a stroke against the Philistines — "the Spirit of the LORD came upon him, and he went down to Ashkelon, and slew thirty men... and gave change of garments unto them": he paid his wager from the spoil of the enemy, and so the very loss became an occasion of smiting the Philistines, as God intended; the using of the affront to begin the deliverance shows how God turns men's wrongs to his purpose; it teaches that the LORD overrules the malice of the wicked to advance his own ends, the spoiler spoiled, "he taketh the wise in their own craftiness" (Job 5:13), the Spirit moving Samson to slay at Ashkelon.

So the witnesses make the verse slew thirty at Ashkelon: The witnesses note the the Spirit came upon him: empowered. The witnesses note the went down to Ashkelon: the stroke. The witnesses note the slew thirty men: took their spoil. The witnesses note the the affront overruled: he taketh the wise in their craftiness (Job 5:13). The expositors observe the the Spirit came upon him: empowered. The expositors observe the went down to Ashkelon: the stroke. The expositors observe the slew thirty men: took their spoil. The expositors observe the the affront overruled: he taketh the wise in their craftiness (Job 5:13). The believer is taught to mark the the Spirit came upon him, the went down to Ashkelon, the slew thirty men, the the affront overruled, and to beware the snare of the unequal yoke. The verse thus records that the Spirit moved Samson to slay thirty at Ashkelon and pay the wager from the enemy's spoil — God turning the affront into a stroke against the Philistines, overruling the malice of the wicked, taking the wise in their own craftiness (Job 5:13).
20:
"But Samson's wife was given to his companion, whom he had used as his friend." The witnesses note Samson's wife given to his companion, the fruit of the unequal alliance. The witnesses note that Gill notes she was given by her father, with her consent, both being provoked by Samson leaving her. The witnesses note that she was given to his companion, used as his friend. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the Samson's wife — given away; the to his companion — his false friend; the the unequal match — its bitter fruit; the betrayed by the trusted — mine own friend lifted up his heel (Ps. 41:9).

The witnesses agree the chapter closes with the bitter fruit of the unequal match — "Samson's wife was given to his companion": the woman he had so desired was given to another, and his friend proved false; the faithlessness of the Philistine wife and friend shows the sorrow that springs from alliance with the ungodly; it teaches that the unequal yoke ends in grief and betrayal, the believer wounded by those he trusted among the enemy, "mine own familiar friend... hath lifted up his heel against me" (Ps. 41:9); thus the marriage at Timnath came to nothing but trouble, Samson's wife given to his companion.

So the witnesses make the verse given to his companion: The witnesses note the Samson's wife: given away. The witnesses note the to his companion: his false friend. The witnesses note the the unequal match: its bitter fruit. The witnesses note the betrayed by the trusted: mine own friend lifted up his heel (Ps. 41:9). The expositors observe the Samson's wife: given away. The expositors observe the to his companion: his false friend. The expositors observe the the unequal match: its bitter fruit. The expositors observe the betrayed by the trusted: mine own friend lifted up his heel (Ps. 41:9). The believer is taught to mark the Samson's wife, the to his companion, the the unequal match, the betrayed by the trusted, and to beware the snare of the unequal yoke. The verse thus records that Samson's wife was given to his companion — the bitter fruit of the unequal alliance, sorrow and betrayal springing from union with the ungodly, mine own familiar friend lifting up his heel against me (Ps. 41:9).
